tweets.full_text:
The fox in the henhouse paradox
A simple question every executive driving an enterprise AI transformation should be asking.
When you deploy OpenAI, Anthropic, or any other model provider directly at the core of your operations, who captures the value of that usage?

The answer is worth a pause.

Your queries feed the continuous training and improvement of their models. 

Your use cases inform their product roadmaps. 

Your volumes fund their next generations. 

And in parallel, those same providers are launching or funding vertical applications that compete head-on with your own business lines.
This is not a failure on their part. 
It is the normal mechanics of a player maximizing its position. 
It is a failure on the part of whoever lets that mechanism run without a counterweight.
The layer that separates usage from the provider
The real question is not whether to pick Anthropic, OpenAI, Mistral, or Gemini. 
The real question is: who arbitrates token allocation across these providers, based on what criteria, and with what level of traceability?
An organization that owns this arbitration layer keeps three decisive levers.
First, it can route every use case to the most performant and cost-effective model at any given moment, without locking contractual dependencies. 
The foundation model market is commoditizing at accelerating speed. 
Performance gaps between equivalent models are now measured in weeks, not years. 
Without an arbitration layer, you keep paying yesterday's leader's price.
Second, it retains ownership of its usage data, its business prompts, its agentic workflows. 
These assets are not peripheral. 
They are the codified expression of your operational know-how. 
Handing them over in plain text to a third-party provider amounts to outsourcing your competitive edge to the actor best positioned to replicate it.
Third, it can document end to end what is requested, from which model, with which data, for which outcome. Eight months out from full EU AI Act enforcement, this traceability shifts from convenience to regulatory obligation. 
Organizations in regulated sectors operating without such a governance layer will find themselves non-compliant by construction.
The shift in value
The thesis deserves to be stated plainly. 
Value in enterprise AI no longer concentrates on the models themselves. It is migrating to the integration, alignment, and governance layer that separates business usage from raw capacity providers.
It is this layer that allows a bank, an insurer, an industrial operator, or a pharmaceutical company to:
– Keep control of its technical choices without being subject to its providers' strategic pivots. 
– Orchestrate AI agents within an auditable and compliant framework. 
– Build a capital of proprietary prompts, workflows, and knowledge that appreciates over time instead of dissolving into third-party models.
The organizations that accept disruption are the ones standing still. 
Worse: the ones actively accelerating their own disruption by integrating, without counterweight, the very players whose mission is to replace them.
A governance question, not a technology one
This is not an architect's debate. It is a board decision.
He who controls the tokens controls the spice.
And he who controls the spice controls the table.

At HIKARI BLUE, we work with executive committees at financial institutions, insurers, and regulated industrial groups to build this control layer before the EU AI Act makes it mandatory.
If your board's next agenda includes your exposure to model providers, I am opening a few confidential diagnostic slots this month. 30 minutes, no commitment, to map your position.
DM

tweets.full_text:
Lawyers charge $500 an hour to review contracts.
Anthropic just launched Claude for Legal inside Microsoft Word.
You can now offer contract review for $150.
Here are 5 things I learned building this service.

Lesson 1: Claude for Legal launched May 12, 2026.

It's a Word plugin.
You install it. Connect your Claude account.
Open any contract. Claude reads it live in the document.
It flags clauses, suggests changes, and writes tracked edits directly into Word.
Everything shows up as standard Word tracked changes the client can accept or reject.

Lesson 2: You don't need a law degree to offer document review.

Document preparation services are legal in every US state.
You're not giving legal advice.
You're preparing a document based on standard market terms.
Paralegals have done this for decades.
Claude just makes you faster than any paralegal.

Lesson 3: The service that sells fastest.

NDA review — $97.
Service agreement review — $147.
Freelance contract review — $127.
Employment offer letter review — $97.

Turnaround: 24 hours.
Actual time: 20 minutes per document.
Claude flags every deviation from standard market terms.
You write a one-paragraph summary of the issues.
You deliver the marked-up Word file.

Lesson 4: The prompt that powers the whole thing.

Paste the contract into Claude with this:
"You are a senior contracts attorney.
Review this agreement and identify:
1. Any clauses that deviate from standard market terms
2. Missing protective clauses the reviewer should have
3. Any liability traps or one-sided provisions
4. Specific language changes — show old and new text.
Flag each issue as critical, moderate, or informational."

Claude delivers a structured review in under 60 seconds.
You copy the tracked changes into Word.
Done.

Lesson 5: The number that closes the sale.

"A lawyer charges $500/hour for this.
I charge $147. Same level of review. 24-hour turnaround."

That pitch converts.
Every founder, freelancer, and small business owner has a contract sitting in their inbox they don't understand and can't afford to have reviewed.

You're the person who solves that.
Claude is the tool that makes it possible.

Save this.

Comment LEGAL for a free book.

tweets.full_text:
Dario and Daniela if you count them as EAs, which we probably should given the extent of social ties.

SBF, but he’s no longer active.

Holden, but now he’s at Anthropic.

Matheny, but he’s less relevant under Trump. Also Toner became less relevant after the board stuff went down.

Buck and Paul became more marginalist thinkers over this period, which is pretty opposed to live player status (e.g. Buck’s control stuff is explicitly marginalist). Carl was maybe always marginalist but I don’t really understand him.

Other live players like Vassar, Yudkowsky, Habryka are more rationalist.

My sense is that the Oxford folks were mostly downstream of the Bay Area consensus-setting done by the folks above (and maybe some others I’m forgetting).

user.name: Sascha Theismann
user.description: Over a decade Market Research experience: voice of the customer / social media / digital transformation / business innovation / NewWork https://t.co/lB5jjqx4o8

tweets.full_text:
AI 操控 CAD 制图，这条路最近真的能跑通了。

主力方案是 MCP（Model Context Protocol）。让 Claude 这类模型直接接管 CAD 软件，自然语言进，图纸出。

现在能用的：

• Autodesk Fusion MCP，Autodesk 和 Anthropic 官方合作，2026 年 4 月底那批 connector 之一，限 Fusion 订阅用户。

• AutoCAD MCP（puran-water/autocad-mcp），专攻 P&ID 自动化，可对接 CTO 商业符号库，覆盖 600+ ISA 5.1 标准符号。

• FreeCAD MCP，完全开源，入门门槛最低。

• Blender MCP，社区版（ahujasid）21k+ star，官方版也并入了 Anthropic 4 月那批 connector。

• CAD-MCP，一套打通 AutoCAD、中望、浩辰。

• 其他社区版本：SolidWorks、Onshape、KiCad、OpenSCAD。

输入“在 (100,100) 插一台离心泵 P-101，出口接 6 寸闸阀连到 V-201”，AI 直接出 P&ID。

工程师的重复劳动，真的在被重写。
